title: Vendor Invoice and Email Fraud
description: Test your ability to recognize payment fraud, suspicious vendor emails, and risky invoice requests.

questions.prompt: Why is vendor invoice fraud considered a cybersecurity issue?

choices.text: Because printers often break during accounting work
Because attackers may use fake emails, impersonation, or compromised accounts to steal money
Because only IT staff can approve invoices
Because invoices always contain malware

questions.explanation: Vendor payment fraud is often cyber-enabled through email impersonation, account compromise, or social engineering.

questions.prompt: It is safe to update vendor banking information based only on an email if the message looks professional.

choices.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Professional-looking emails can still be fraudulent. Payment changes should be verified through a trusted separate channel.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the biggest red flag in a vendor payment message?

choices.text: It references a real vendor name
It asks you to verify new banking details through a known phone number
It urgently requests a change to payment instructions
It includes an invoice number

questions.explanation: Urgent requests involving payment changes are a major warning sign and should always be verified.

questions.prompt: A message appears to come from a trusted vendor but the email address is slightly different than usual. What should you do?

choices.text: Process the request if the logo looks correct
Reply and ask the sender if they are legitimate
Verify the request using a known contact method before taking action
Ignore the difference because small changes are normal

questions.explanation: Look-alike email addresses are a common fraud tactic. Use a trusted phone number or known vendor contact to verify.

questions.prompt: Which employee groups are commonly targeted by vendor invoice fraud?

choices.text: Only software developers
Finance, operations, purchasing, or anyone handling payments
Only external auditors
Only front desk staff

questions.explanation: Attackers often target employees who handle invoices, purchasing, payroll, or vendor communication.

questions.prompt: A familiar display name in an email always proves the sender is legitimate.

choices.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Display names can be spoofed. Always check the actual sender address and verify sensitive requests.

questions.prompt: Scenario: You receive an email saying a long-time vendor has changed banks and needs today's payment sent to a new account immediately. What is the safest next step?

choices.text: Pay immediately to avoid a late fee
Reply to the email and ask if the new account is correct
Call the vendor using a known trusted number and verify the change before sending payment
Forward the email to coworkers and ask what they think

questions.explanation: Payment changes should be verified outside of email using a trusted contact method.

questions.prompt: Why is urgency so effective in payment fraud scams?

choices.text: It makes invoices easier to read
It pressures employees to act before verifying
It automatically confirms bank details
It improves email security

questions.explanation: Attackers use urgency to reduce careful thinking and push people into skipping verification steps.

questions.prompt: What should you do if you suspect a vendor invoice or payment request is fraudulent?

choices.text: Delete it without telling anyone
Process it later when less busy
Report it through your organization’s process and verify before acting
Send a test payment first

questions.explanation: Suspicious payment messages should be reported and verified before any money is sent.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the best protection against vendor email fraud?

choices.text: Trusting known names automatically
Handling payment changes only through email
Using a separate verification process for financial requests
Opening all attachments to inspect them manually

questions.explanation: A separate verification process is one of the strongest protections against business email and payment fraud.
